advantages: A single scalar η gives a simple, interpretable knob for controlling convergence speed across all parameters.
Well-chosen η enables fast initial loss reduction while keeping updates within the valid local linear region of the loss.
Schedules that reduce η over time combine early exploration with late fine-grained convergence without changing the optimizer.
Log-scale sweeps over η quickly reveal workable ranges before tuning other hyperparameters.
disadvantages: One global η cannot match every direction's curvature on ill-conditioned or non-convex loss surfaces.
η interacts with batch size, gradient noise, and weight scale—optimal values rarely transfer blindly across models.
Too-large η causes catastrophic divergence; too-small η wastes compute and can mask other bugs in long flat curves.
Fixed η without scheduling may plateau early or oscillate late unless paired with adaptive methods or manual decay.
